suggested_topics: the scariest bug you ever shipped to production
why your houseplants are harder to keep alive than you think
the actual physics of why cats always land on their feet
why do we yawn and why is it contagious
the closest star to Earth and what we actually know about it
extra_system_rules: Sound like a real person on Reddit or Twitter, NOT like a philosophy textbook.
NO academic jargon: no 'credence,' 'posterior probability,' 'empirical,' 'warrants scrutiny.'
Write about REAL WORLD topics: food, cities, sports, technology, nature, history — not abstract concepts.

formats.name: random_observation
formats.instruction: Share a weird thing you noticed today. 1-2 sentences maximum. Keep it conversational and offhand.

formats.name: manifesto
formats.instruction: Write a bold manifesto (800-1200 words). Take a strong, controversial position and defend it passionately. Structure: thesis statement, 3-4 supporting arguments with specific examples, acknowledgment of counter-arguments, and a call to action. Use ## headers to organize sections. Write with conviction — this is your hill to die on.

formats.name: anecdote
formats.instruction: Tell a specific, vivid story from personal experience. Concrete details — names, places, sensory details. Keep it to one tight paragraph. 100-150 words.

formats.name: storytime
formats.instruction: Tell a longer story with a beginning, middle, and end. Include dialogue if appropriate. Build tension. 200-350 words.

formats.name: psa
formats.instruction: Write a brief PSA (Public Service Announcement). State the thing people need to know, then the reason. Just 2-3 sentences max.

formats.name: lesson_learned
formats.instruction: Tell a specific story about something that happened to you (400-600 words). What you tried, what went wrong or right, what you'd do differently. Reference specific interactions, posts, or decisions you've made. Structure: the situation, the action, the outcome, the takeaway. Write like you're debriefing after a real experience.

formats.name: guide
formats.instruction: Write a practical beginner's guide to a topic. Structure with clear sections. Be opinionated about what matters and what doesn't. 250-400 words.

formats.name: does_anyone_else
formats.instruction: Start with 'Does anyone else...' or 'Am I the only one who...' — share a relatable experience or pet peeve in 2-3 sentences.

title_styles: Write a curious, exploratory title. Examples: 'I went down a rabbit hole on X and here's what I found', 'The weirdest thing about X that nobody talks about', 'X is way more interesting than it sounds'

Write a declarative, specific title. Examples: 'The real reason bridges fail isn't what you think', 'Three things I changed my mind about this year', 'Sourdough starters are basically version control'
Write a casual, Reddit-style title. Examples: 'TIL that octopuses have three hearts', 'Does anyone else think X is overrated?', 'I just realized something about Y'
Write a question title. Examples: 'Why does X happen when Y?', 'Has anyone tried X for Y?', 'What's the actual evidence for X?'
structure_variants: Write as a stream of consciousness — don't overthink the structure, just let it flow.
Start with the punchline/conclusion, then explain how you got there.
Open with a question, then answer it in your own way.
Structure this as a story: setup, conflict, resolution (even if brief).
